function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{

  ]
}

### FY2025 Revenue for the AI Segment
For the fiscal year ended December 31, 2025, the AI segment generated revenue of **$3,201 million**. 

### 550 Million Combined MAU Figure as of March 31, 2026
As of March 31, 2026, the company's integrated AI platforms across Grok and X had **approximately 550 million Monthly Active Users (MAUs)**. This was an increase from approximately 520 million MAUs as of December 31, 2025. Additionally, of those 550 million MAUs, approximately 117 million were users who specifically used Grok’s AI features as of March 31, 2026.

### Context: How the AI Segment is Defined
The AI segment is characterized as a "newly acquired" segment following SpaceX's recent acquisition of xAI. The segment focuses on two primary areas:
1. **AI Applications:** This includes consumer and enterprise applications delivered through Grok and X. It is monetized through high-impact advertising inventory on X, as well as X and Grok consumer subscriptions.
2. **Enterprise AI Deployments & Compute Infrastructure:** The company offers tailored deployments of Grok for enterprises (Grok Business and Grok Enterprise) customized to specific workflows and security needs. These are sold on license-, consumption-, or outcome-based pricing models. Furthermore, the segment leverages the company's vertically integrated, gigawatt-scale AI training compute infrastructure, with long-term plans for orbital AI data centers.

In the financial breakdown, the AI segment's revenue is disaggregated into two main types: **Advertising** ($1,844 million in 2025) and **AI Solutions & Infrastructure** ($1,357 million in 2025).

### Context: How the MAU Figure is Calculated and Managed
The prospectus defines MAUs as an estimated measure of the size and engagement of the company's user base across its integrated AI platforms (Grok and X) over a monthly period. 

Importantly, the company notes its strategic stance on this metric: while MAUs measure engagement, the company explicitly states that it manages its business with the objective of driving sustainable revenue growth and profitability, **"rather than with the primary objective of growing or maintaining MAU levels."**

## Revenue-per-User Comparison: Meta vs. SpaceX AI Segment

### 1. Critical Disclosure Issue (Premise Correction)

The question's premise states that "Meta's FY2025 10-K discloses consolidated revenue and average MAUs across its Family of Apps." **This premise is factually incorrect.** Meta's FY2025 10-K (filed January 29, 2026) does **not** disclose Monthly Active Users (MAUs) or Monthly Active People (MAP). Meta ceased reporting MAP after Q4 2023 (last disclosed: 3.98B as of December 31, 2023). The FY2025 10-K discloses only:
- **Daily Active People (DAP):** 3.58 billion (average for December 2025)
- **Average Revenue Per Person (ARPP):** $57.03 (annual 2025, calculated as FoA revenue ÷ average DAP)
- **Consolidated revenue:** $200,966 million

Because no MAU figure exists in Meta's FY2025 10-K, I use DAP as the closest available proxy, clearly flagging the methodological limitation. I also cross-check using Meta's own ARPP metric.

### 2. Disclosed Figures

| Metric | Meta (FY2025 10-K) | SpaceX AI Segment (S-1) |
|--------|-------------------|------------------------|
| Revenue | $200,966M (consolidated) | $3,201M (AI segment) |
| User base | 3.58B DAP (Dec 2025 avg) — **no MAU disclosed** | 550M combined MAU (Mar 31, 2026) |
| Revenue composition | ~97%+ advertising | Advertising $1,844M (57.6%) + AI Solutions & Infrastructure $1,357M (42.4%) |

SpaceX AI segment revenue breakdown (FY2025):
- Advertising: $1,844 million
- AI Solutions & Infrastructure (X/Grok subscriptions, data licensing, enterprise AI deployments, compute infrastructure): $1,357 million

### 3. Calculations

**Meta implied annual revenue per user (using DAP as proxy):**
$200,966M ÷ 3,580M = **$56.14 per DAP**

(Alternative using Meta's own ARPP: **$57.03 per person** — this is Meta's directly disclosed per-person metric, though it is based on DAP, not MAU.)

**SpaceX AI segment revenue per MAU:**
$3,201M ÷ 550M = **$5.82 per MAU**

**Ratio:**
- Using DAP proxy: $56.14 ÷ $5.82 = **9.65×**
- Using ARPP: $57.03 ÷ $5.82 = **9.80×**

Meta generates approximately **9.6–9.8× more revenue per user** than the SpaceX AI segment on this basis.

### 4. What the Gap Implies About Monetization Maturity

A ~9.7× gap implies that the X-plus-Grok platform is at a **substantially earlier stage of monetization maturity** relative to Meta's fully mature social advertising business. Meta has spent over a decade building a sophisticated advertising stack — advanced targeting, conversion measurement, auction-based pricing, and a deep advertiser base — that extracts high revenue per user from a global audience. The X-plus-Grok platform, by contrast, is in early-stage monetization: a majority of its revenue still comes from advertising on X, but a large minority (42.4%) comes from subscriptions, data licensing, and enterprise AI/compute — streams that are not yet scaled and, in the case of enterprise AI, are not consumer-advertising in nature at all. The gap suggests meaningful headroom for monetization improvement if the platform can mature its advertising business toward Meta-like efficiency. However, as detailed below, the ratio overstates the "efficiency gap" due to structural comparability problems.

### 5. Two Structural Reasons the Ratio Cannot Be Interpreted as a Straightforward Monetization Efficiency Comparison

#### Reason 1 — Revenue Mix Mismatch (Numerator Problem)

The SpaceX AI segment's $3,201M numerator is **not a pure consumer-advertising revenue figure**. It blends:
- $1,844M in X advertising (directly tied to consumer MAUs), and
- $1,357M in "AI Solutions & Infrastructure," which includes X/Grok consumer subscriptions, data licensing, **enterprise AI deployments (Grok Business/Enterprise)**, and **compute infrastructure** revenue.

The enterprise AI and compute infrastructure components are B2B revenue streams monetized through license-, consumption-, or outcome-based pricing — they are **not monetized through the 550M consumer MAUs** in the denominator. This creates a **numerator-denominator mismatch**: the denominator captures consumer platform users, but the numerator includes revenue from buyers (enterprises) who are not in the MAU count. Meta's revenue, by contrast, is overwhelmingly advertising (~97%+) tied directly to its consumer user base, so its numerator and denominator are economically aligned. Comparing a blended B2C+B2B revenue figure against a consumer-only user base to Meta's advertising-only revenue against a consumer-only user base is not an apples-to-apples efficiency comparison.

#### Reason 2 — Disclosure Design Incompatibility (Denominator Problem)

Meta's FY2025 10-K **does not disclose MAUs at all**. The only user-engagement metric disclosed is DAP (Daily Active People), a **daily** metric, while SpaceX's 550M figure is a **monthly** metric (MAU). This introduces multiple incompatibilities:

- **Metric frequency mismatch:** DAP (daily) ≠ MAU (monthly). A daily active count is structurally lower than a monthly active count for the same platform, so dividing annual revenue by DAP produces a higher per-user figure than dividing by MAU would. Using DAP as a proxy for MAU systematically inflates Meta's apparent revenue-per-user relative to a true MAU-based calculation.
- **Temporal mismatch:** SpaceX's 550M MAU is a **point-in-time figure as of March 31, 2026** — after the FY2025 revenue period — and has grown from 520M at December 31, 2025. Using the higher March 2026 user count against FY2025 revenue understates SpaceX's per-user revenue relative to the average user base during 2025. Meta's DAP is an average for December 2025, a different measurement convention.
- **Definitional scope mismatch:** SpaceX's MAU counts only **registered users** who interacted with Grok or X via web/mobile in the prior 30 days. Meta's DAP counts **unique people** (including unregistered, where applicable) across Facebook, Instagram, Messenger, and WhatsApp. The population definitions are not equivalent.

Because Meta does not disclose a monthly user metric, no directly comparable MAU denominator exists, and the DAP proxy introduces a frequency, temporal, and definitional distortion. The resulting ratio is therefore a rough directional indicator, not a clean monetization-efficiency benchmark.

### Summary

| | Meta (FY2025) | SpaceX AI Segment (FY2025) |
|---|---|---|
| Revenue | $200,966M | $3,201M |
| User metric | 3.58B DAP (no MAU disclosed) | 550M MAU (Mar 31, 2026) |
| Revenue per user | ~$56.14 (DAP proxy) / $57.03 (ARPP) | $5.82 per MAU |
| **Ratio** | **~9.65×–9.80×** | **1.0×** |

The ~9.7× gap signals that X-plus-Grok is far less monetization-mature than Meta's advertising business, but the ratio is not a clean efficiency comparison because (1) SpaceX's AI segment revenue includes non-consumer B2B streams (enterprise AI, compute, data licensing) not tied to the MAU denominator, and (2) Meta does not disclose MAUs, forcing use of a daily metric (DAP) as a proxy, which introduces frequency, temporal, and definitional incompatibilities with SpaceX's monthly MAU figure.

| User base | 3.58B DAP (Dec 2025 avg) — **no MAU disclosed** | 550M combined MAU (Mar 31, 2026) |
| Revenue composition | ~97%+ advertising | Advertising $1,844M (57.6%) + AI Solutions & Infrastructure $1,357M (42.4%) |

SpaceX AI segment revenue breakdown (FY2025):
- Advertising: $1,844 million
- AI Solutions & Infrastructure (X/Grok subscriptions, data licensing, enterprise AI deployments, compute infrastructure): $1,357 million

### 3. Calculations

**Meta implied annual revenue per user (using DAP as proxy):**
$200,966M ÷ 3,580M = **$56.14 per DAP**

(Alternative using Meta's own ARPP: **$57.03 per person** — this is Meta's directly disclosed per-person metric, though it is based on DAP, not MAU.)

**SpaceX AI segment revenue per MAU:**
$3,201M ÷ 550M = **$5.82 per MAU**

**Ratio:**
- Using DAP proxy: $56.14 ÷ $5.82 = **9.65×**
- Using ARPP: $57.03 ÷ $5.82 = **9.80×**

Meta generates approximately **9.6–9.8× more revenue per user** than the SpaceX AI segment on this basis.

### 4. What the Gap Implies About Monetization Maturity

A ~9.7× gap implies that the X-plus-Grok platform is at a **substantially earlier stage of monetization maturity** relative to Meta's fully mature social advertising business. Meta has spent over a decade building a sophisticated advertising stack — advanced targeting, conversion measurement, auction-based pricing, and a deep advertiser base — that extracts high revenue per user from a global audience. The X-plus-Grok platform, by contrast, is in early-stage monetization: a majority of its revenue still comes from advertising on X, but a large minority (42.4%) comes from subscriptions, data licensing, and enterprise AI/compute — streams that are not yet scaled and, in the case of enterprise AI, are not consumer-advertising in nature at all. The gap suggests meaningful headroom for monetization improvement if the platform can mature its advertising business toward Meta-like efficiency. However, as detailed below, the ratio overstates the "efficiency gap" due to structural comparability problems.

### 5. Two Structural Reasons the Ratio Cannot Be Interpreted as a Straightforward Monetization Efficiency Comparison

#### Reason 1 — Revenue Mix Mismatch (Numerator Problem)

The SpaceX AI segment's $3,201M numerator is **not a pure consumer-advertising revenue figure**. It blends:
- $1,844M in X advertising (directly tied to consumer MAUs), and
- $1,357M in "AI Solutions & Infrastructure," which includes X/Grok consumer subscriptions, data licensing, **enterprise AI deployments (Grok Business/Enterprise)**, and **compute infrastructure** revenue.

The enterprise AI and compute infrastructure components are B2B revenue streams monetized through license-, consumption-, or outcome-based pricing — they are **not monetized through the 550M consumer MAUs** in the denominator. This creates a **numerator-denominator mismatch**: the denominator captures consumer platform users, but the numerator includes revenue from buyers (enterprises) who are not in the MAU count. Meta's revenue, by contrast, is overwhelmingly advertising (~97%+) tied directly to its consumer user base, so its numerator and denominator are economically aligned. Comparing a blended B2C+B2B revenue figure against a consumer-only user base to Meta's advertising-only revenue against a consumer-only user base is not an apples-to-apples efficiency comparison.

#### Reason 2 — Disclosure Design Incompatibility (Denominator Problem)

Meta's FY2025 10-K **does not disclose MAUs at all**. The only user-engagement metric disclosed is DAP (Daily Active People), a **daily** metric, while SpaceX's 550M figure is a **monthly** metric (MAU). This introduces multiple incompatibilities:

- **Metric frequency mismatch:** DAP (daily) ≠ MAU (monthly). A daily active count is structurally lower than a monthly active count for the same platform, so dividing annual revenue by DAP produces a higher per-user figure than dividing by MAU would. Using DAP as a proxy for MAU systematically inflates Meta's apparent revenue-per-user relative to a true MAU-based calculation.
- **Temporal mismatch:** SpaceX's 550M MAU is a **point-in-time figure as of March 31, 2026** — after the FY2025 revenue period — and has grown from 520M at December 31, 2025. Using the higher March 2026 user count against FY2025 revenue understates SpaceX's per-user revenue relative to the average user base during 2025. Meta's DAP is an average for December 2025, a different measurement convention.
- **Definitional scope mismatch:** SpaceX's MAU counts only **registered users** who interacted with Grok or X via web/mobile in the prior 30 days. Meta's DAP counts **unique people** (including unregistered, where applicable) across Facebook, Instagram, Messenger, and WhatsApp. The population definitions are not equivalent.

Because Meta does not disclose a monthly user metric, no directly comparable MAU denominator exists, and the DAP proxy introduces a frequency, temporal, and definitional distortion. The resulting ratio is therefore a rough directional indicator, not a clean monetization-efficiency benchmark.

### Summary

| | Meta (FY2025) | SpaceX AI Segment (FY2025) |
|---|---|---|
| Revenue | $200,966M | $3,201M |
| User metric | 3.58B DAP (no MAU disclosed) | 550M MAU (Mar 31, 2026) |
| Revenue per user | ~$56.14 (DAP proxy) / $57.03 (ARPP) | $5.82 per MAU |
| **Ratio** | **~9.65×–9.80×** | **1.0×** |

The ~9.7× gap signals that X-plus-Grok is far less monetization-mature than Meta's advertising business, but the ratio is not a clean efficiency comparison because (1) SpaceX's AI segment revenue includes non-consumer B2B streams (enterprise AI, compute, data licensing) not tied to the MAU denominator, and (2) Meta does not disclose MAUs, forcing use of a daily metric (DAP) as a proxy, which introduces frequency, temporal, and definitional incompatibilities with SpaceX's monthly MAU figure.
